From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Online speculative fiction magazine

*Strange Horizons*

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | |
| Editor-in-chief | Gautam Bhatia |
| Former editors | Jane Crowley Kate Dollarhyde [Niall Harrison](/wiki/Niall_Harrison "Niall Harrison") Susan Marie Groppi [Mary Anne Mohanraj](/wiki/Mary_Anne_Mohanraj "Mary Anne Mohanraj") |
| Categories | [Speculative fiction](/wiki/Speculative_fiction "Speculative fiction") |
| Frequency | Weekly |
| Founder | [Mary Anne Mohanraj](/wiki/Mary_Anne_Mohanraj "Mary Anne Mohanraj") |
| First issue | September 2000 (2000-09) |
| Based in | Utah |
| Language | English |
| Website | [strangehorizons.com](http://strangehorizons.com) |
| [OCLC](/wiki/OCLC_(identifier) "OCLC (identifier)") | [56474213](https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/56474213) |

***Strange Horizons*** is an [online](/wiki/Online_magazine "Online magazine") [speculative fiction](/wiki/Speculative_fiction "Speculative fiction") magazine. It also features [speculative poetry](/wiki/Speculative_poetry "Speculative poetry") and [non-fiction](/wiki/Non-fiction "Non-fiction") in every issue, including reviews, essays, interviews, and roundtables.

## History and profile

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Strange_Horizons&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History and profile")]

It was launched in September 2000, and publishes new material (fiction, articles, reviews, poetry, and/or art) 51 weeks of the year, with an emphasis on "new, underrepresented, and global voices."[[1]](#cite_note-Fond_Farewells-1) The magazine was founded by writer and editor [Mary Anne Mohanraj](/wiki/Mary_Anne_Mohanraj "Mary Anne Mohanraj").[[2]](#cite_note-The_Guardian_says_SH_nurtures_new_SF_writers-2) It is registered with the [IRS](/wiki/Internal_Revenue_Service "Internal Revenue Service") as [501(c)(3)](/wiki/501(c)(3)_organization "501(c)(3) organization") [non-profit organization](/wiki/Nonprofit_organization "Nonprofit organization").[[3]](#cite_note-3) It has a staff of approximately sixty volunteers, and is unusual among professional speculative fiction magazines in being funded entirely by donations, holding annual fund drives.

## Editors-in-chief

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Strange_Horizons&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Editors-in-chief")]

* [Mary Anne Mohanraj](/wiki/Mary_Anne_Mohanraj "Mary Anne Mohanraj"), 2000–2003
* Susan Marie Groppi, 2004–2010
* [Niall Harrison](/wiki/Niall_Harrison "Niall Harrison"), 2010–2017[[4]](#cite_note-4)
* Jane Crowley and Kate Dollarhyde, 2017–2019[[5]](#cite_note-5)
* Vanessa Rose Phin, 2019–2021[[6]](#cite_note-6)
* [Gautam Bhatia](/wiki/Gautam_Bhatia_(lawyer) "Gautam Bhatia (lawyer)"), 2021–present[[7]](#cite_note-7)

## Awards

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Strange_Horizons&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Awards")]

Susan Marie Groppi won the [World Fantasy Special Award—Non-professional](/wiki/World_Fantasy_Special_Award%E2%80%94Non-professional "World Fantasy Special Award—Non-professional") in 2010 for her work as Editor-in-Chief on *Strange Horizons*.[[8]](#cite_note-8) The magazine itself was a finalist for the [Hugo Award for Best Website](/wiki/Discontinued_Hugo_Awards "Discontinued Hugo Awards") in 2002[[9]](#cite_note-9) and 2005.[[10]](#cite_note-10) The magazine won the [Hugo Award for Best Semiprozine](/wiki/Hugo_Award_for_Best_Semiprozine "Hugo Award for Best Semiprozine") in 2024, after being a finalist every year from 2013 onward. *Strange Horizons* won The Community Award for Outstanding Efforts in Service of Inclusion and Equitable Practice in Genre, presented by the [Ignyte Awards](/wiki/Ignyte_Awards "Ignyte Awards"), in 2020.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

The short story "[The House Beyond Your Sky](/wiki/The_House_Beyond_Your_Sky "The House Beyond Your Sky")" by [Benjamin Rosenbaum](/wiki/Benjamin_Rosenbaum "Benjamin Rosenbaum"), published in 2006[[12]](#cite_note-12) in the magazine, was nominated for a 2007 [Hugo Award for Best Short Story](/wiki/Hugo_Award_for_Best_Short_Story "Hugo Award for Best Short Story").[[13]](#cite_note-13) "[Selkie Stories Are for Losers](/wiki/Selkie_Stories_Are_for_Losers "Selkie Stories Are for Losers")" by [Sofia Samatar](/wiki/Sofia_Samatar "Sofia Samatar") was nominated for a Hugo Award for Best Short Story in 2014. Other stories in *Strange Horizons* have been nominated for the Nebula and other awards.[[14]](#cite_note-14) Three stories published in *Strange Horizons* have won the [Theodore Sturgeon Award](/wiki/Theodore_Sturgeon_Award "Theodore Sturgeon Award").
